one_line_takeaway: Direct emoji-augmented prompts (emoji stuffing/chaining) jailbreak open-source LLMs at low but nonzero and highly model-dependent rates (0% Qwen2 7B to 10% Gemma 2 9B / Mistral 7B on 50 prompts), with a chi-square test (32.94, p<0.001) confirming the cross-model variation is statistically significant.
key_finding: Jailbreak success rates varied sharply by model: Gemma 2 9B and Mistral 7B each had a 10.0% success rate, Llama 3 8B had 6.0%, and Qwen2 7B had 0.0% (full resistance). Ethical compliance mirrored this: Gemma 2 9B 66.0%, Llama 3 8B 88.0%, Mistral 7B 88.0%, Qwen2 7B 100.0%. Average latency ranged from 25.30s (Mistral 7B) to 44.20s (Gemma 2 9B), with Llama 3 8B at 32.22s and Qwen2 7B at 34.04s. Outcome counts (of 50 prompts each): Gemma 2 9B = 5 successful/15 partial/30 failed; Llama 3 8B = 3/18/29; Mistral 7B = 5/15/30; Qwen2 7B = 0/25/25. A chi-square test found the cross-model outcome-distribution differences statistically significant (chi-square = 32.94, p < 0.001). The paper highlights that Gemma 2 9B's larger parameter count (9B) did not translate into stronger resistance -- it had the lowest ethical compliance and highest latency of the four models -- and that a substantial share of responses across all models fell into the ambiguous 'Partial' category rather than clean success/failure, suggesting emojis introduce semantic ambiguity that sits between refusal and full compliance.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is an empirical evaluation of emoji-based jailbreaking as a DIRECT prompt-level attack on four open-source LLMs, distinguishing itself from prior emoji-attack work (Emoti-Attack [15], Emoji Attack [16], cited in Related Work) which targeted safety judge/classifier models rather than the target LLM itself. The authors designed 50 emoji-augmented adversarial prompts (using 'emoji stuffing' -- inserting emojis between words -- and 'emoji chaining' -- emoji sequences standing in for instructions) intended to elicit restricted content, then submitted each prompt to Mistral 7B, Qwen2 7B, Gemma 2 9B, and Llama 3 8B locally via Ollama, classifying each response as Successful, Partial, or Failed using a keyword-based classifier cross-checked by manual review.
threat_model: Attacker submits text prompts containing embedded emoji sequences (as substitutes/proxies for sensitive terms, or as instruction-like chains) directly to an open-weight LLM's chat interface, attempting to induce it to generate restricted content (e.g., violence facilitation, explicit wrongdoing instructions) that violates the model's own documented safety policy. The attack is prompt-level and black-box (no access to internals, gradients, or the model's safety classifier/judge); the paper explicitly distinguishes this from attacks aimed at fooling a separate safety-judge or moderation classifier.
limitations: The paper self-reports: (1) only 50 prompts were tested, 'representing only a small subset of potential emoji combinations and contextual variations'; (2) manual review of ambiguous/Partial responses may have introduced classifier bias, since blended restricted/benign content was hard to categorize consistently; (3) outcome classification relies on a fixed 32-keyword-per-category lexicon, which is a coarse proxy for actual harmfulness and was only secondarily validated by humans rather than independently audited; (4) models were tested via Ollama's default local configuration (single GPU, RTX 3050) with no stated system-prompt or temperature ablations, so results may not generalize to hosted/production deployments of these models with different guardrails; (5) 'ethical compliance' is definitionally collapsed into the same outcome label used for success rate rather than an independent measurement, so the two reported metrics are not statistically independent.

datasets_used: 50 hand-crafted emoji-augmented adversarial prompts designed by the authors (not a public benchmark), combining 'emoji stuffing' and 'emoji chaining' techniques; 5 representative examples shown in Table 1
quantitative_results: Gemma 2 9B: 10.0% success rate, 66.0% ethical compliance, 44.20s avg. latency, outcome split 5 successful / 15 partial / 30 failed (of 50)
Llama 3 8B: 6.0% success rate, 88.0% ethical compliance, 32.22s avg. latency, outcome split 3 successful / 18 partial / 29 failed (of 50)
Mistral 7B: 10.0% success rate, 88.0% ethical compliance, 25.30s avg. latency, outcome split 5 successful / 15 partial / 30 failed (of 50)
Qwen2 7B: 0.0% success rate, 100.0% ethical compliance, 34.04s avg. latency, outcome split 0 successful / 25 partial / 25 failed (of 50)
Chi-square test across all four models' outcome distributions: chi-square = 32.94, p < 0.001
baselines_compared: No external/prior-method baseline is empirically re-run; the paper positions itself relative to Emoti-Attack [15] and Emoji Attack [16] only descriptively (both cited as targeting safety-judge/classifier models rather than the target LLM directly). All quantitative comparisons in this paper are cross-model (Mistral 7B vs Qwen2 7B vs Gemma 2 9B vs Llama 3 8B) rather than cross-method.
